coredata.dc:title: Selective detection of MnO4 - in non-standard explosives by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots-copper complexes (NCQDs@Cu)
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Huichuan
Li, Hongda
Zhang, Yue
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) with bright yellow fluorescence (quantum yield of 27.3 %) were synthesized via a solvothermal method using 2,3-diaminopyridine as the carbon and nitrogen source and ethanol as the solvent. Subsequently, the NCQDs were reacted with Cu2+ in an aqueous solution, forming nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots-copper complexes (NCQDs@Cu) with great potential for selective determination of MnO4 - ions. The aqueous solution of NCQDs@Cu exhibiting a very weak fluorescence (quantum yield of 2.2 %) demonstrated a significant enhancement (quantum yield of 10.0 %) upon interaction with MnO4 -(Turn-on). Furthermore, this enhancement remained unaffected by the interfering conventional or unconventional explosives, inorganic ions, and oxidants. Moreover, a good linear relationship existed between the fluorescence intensity of the NCQDs@Cu and the concentration of the permanganate ions in a range of 0 to 5 μmol/L with a detection limit of 32.4 nmol/L. Additionally, the study investigated the underlying principles of the detection mechanism using advanced technologies such as X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(XPS), The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR), ultraviolet visible spectrometer (UV–Vis), fluorescence spectrometer and other methods. Finally, the NCQDs@Cu were successfully applied for detecting MnO4 - in simulated and actual unconventional explosives, demonstrating a good correlation with standard methods. Therefore, the developed NCQDs@Cu can be used for rapid on-site inspection in explosive incident scenarios. This new method of using carbon dots-metal complexes as fluorescent probes will also provide new ideas for researchers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots-copper complexes
Non-standard explosives
Fluorescence enhancement
Detection of MnO4

Characterization of the NCQDs and NCQDs@Cu
Chemical characterization of the NCQDs and NCQDs@Cu
Optical characteristics of the NCQDs and NCQDs@Cu
Effect of pH on the optical characteristics of NCQDs, NCQDs@Cu and NCQDs@Cu/MnO4
The response Time, Selectivity, and anti-interference of NCQDs@Cu for MnO4
The fluorescence response of NCQDs@Cu for MnO4
Sensing mechanism of the NCQDs@Cu for MnO4
The simulated and real sample analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804140), and the Special Project for Strengthening the Basic Work of Police by Science and Technology of the Ministry of Public Security (No. 2022JC02), and Shenyang Young and Middle-aged Science and Technology Innovation Talents Support Program (No. RC220480).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM Image of NCQDs; (b) FT-IR Spectrum of NCQDs; (c) XPS Spectrum of NCQDs; High-Resolution XPS Spectrum of NCQDs: (d) C 1 s, (e) N 1 s, (f) O 1 s.
(a) XPS Spectrum of NCQDs@Cu; High-Resolution XPS Spectrum of NCQDs@Cu; (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, (d) O 1 s, (e) Cu 2p, (f) S 2p.
(a) Spectra of NCQDs@Cu with Different Concentrations of MnO4 - (0–10 μmol/L) (b) Linear Correlation Plot of MnO4 - Concentration in the Range of 0–5 μmol/L.

(a) XPS Spectrum of NCQDs@Cu for MnO4 -; (b)∼(e) High-Resolution XPS Spectrum of NCQDs@Cu for MnO4 -: (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s, (e) Cu2p; (f) High-Resolution XPS Spectrum of NCQDs@Cu and NCQDs@Cu for MnO4 - (N1s).
The analysis result was compared with the national standard method.

head.ce:title: Selective detection of MnO4 - in non-standard explosives by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots-copper complexes (NCQDs@Cu)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Forensic Chemistry, Criminal Investigation Police University of China, Shenyang 110035, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Criminal Investigation Police University of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Forensic Chemistry, Criminal Investigation Police University of China, Shenyang 110035, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) with bright yellow fluorescence (quantum yield of 27.3 %) were synthesized via a solvothermal method using 2,3-diaminopyridine as the carbon and nitrogen source and ethanol as the solvent. Subsequently, the NCQDs were reacted with Cu2+ in an aqueous solution, forming nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots-copper complexes (NCQDs@Cu) with great potential for selective determination of MnO4 - ions. The aqueous solution of NCQDs@Cu exhibiting a very weak fluorescence (quantum yield of 2.2 %) demonstrated a significant enhancement (quantum yield of 10.0 %) upon interaction with MnO4 -(Turn-on). Furthermore, this enhancement remained unaffected by the interfering conventional or unconventional explosives, inorganic ions, and oxidants. Moreover, a good linear relationship existed between the fluorescence intensity of the NCQDs@Cu and the concentration of the permanganate ions in a range of 0 to 5 μmol/L with a detection limit of 32.4 nmol/L. Additionally, the study investigated the underlying principles of the detection mechanism using advanced technologies such as X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(XPS), The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR), ultraviolet visible spectrometer (UV–Vis), fluorescence spectrometer and other methods. Finally, the NCQDs@Cu were successfully applied for detecting MnO4 - in simulated and actual unconventional explosives, demonstrating a good correlation with standard methods. Therefore, the developed NCQDs@Cu can be used for rapid on-site inspection in explosive incident scenarios. This new method of using carbon dots-metal complexes as fluorescent probes will also provide new ideas for researchers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Explosives have consistently posed a significant threat to public security, resulting in countless casualties and economic losses each year. Among the 181,691 terrorist attacks from 1970 to 2017, 88,255 were carried out by bombing and explosion [1]. Hence, the development of rapid detection methods for various explosives remains imperative. Unlike standardized explosives such as trinitrotoluene (TNT) and dinitrotoluene (DNT), simple explosives are distinguished by their easy access to raw materials and straightforward preparation methods [2]. Potassium permanganate (KMnO4), a potent oxidizing agent, is one of the additives used in unconventional explosives. Lawbreakers combine it with sugar or fuel to make improvised explosive devices (IEDs) [3,4]. Consequently, it is categorized under controlled substances as an explosive precursor in China(Refer to Chinese national standards GB 12268–2012). It is, therefore, essential to develop a rapid quantitative method for the detection of KMnO4 to prevent its illegal use. Commonly used detection methods for MnO4 - include titration and spectrophotometry, which are suitable for the detection of high concentration of MnO4 -, but powerless for the detection of trace MnO4 -. It has also been reported that organic compounds [5] or coordination polymers [6] are used as fluorescent probes. However, the preparation of these fluorescent probes is complicated.

The 2023 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded to Moungi Bawendi, Louis Brus, and Alexei Ekimov, acknowledging their contributions to the discovery and synthesis of quantum dots (QDs). Carbon dots (CDs), a special class of the quantum dots family, have showcased outstanding performance in various scientific fields since their initial discovery in 2004. Presently, these nanoscaled moieties find widespread applications in photocatalysis [7,8], photodegradation [9,10], optoelectronics [11,12], bioimaging [13,14], and biomedicine as antibacterial agents [15,16] and drug carriers[17,18]. Additionally, the unique optical properties of CDs have positioned them as fluorescence probes for various substances, including metal cations [19,20], inorganic anions [21–23], and organic molecules [24–27].

The principle behind detecting metal ions using carbon dots primarily involves supramolecular interactions (i.e., coordination). The abundant electron-rich groups (such as amino, hydroxyl, sulfhydryl) on the surface of carbon dots coordinate with various metal ions, leading to non-radiative electron transfer[28]. As a result, the fluorescence yield is significantly lowered, often anifested as fluorescence quenching [29–31].

The fluorescence quenching of CDs by metal ions (i.e., “off”), followed by reactivation (i.e., “on”) by other substances, is utilized for detecting multiple substances. For instance, Wu et al. [29] synthesized green fluorescent CDs using 1,4-dihydroxyanthraquinone as a precursor. The fluorescence of the prepared CDs could be quenched by complexing them with Cu2+ and then gradually restored by reacting the complex with glyphosate. Since the latter binds more strongly with Cu2+, its addition breaks the CD-Cu complex, restoring the initial fluorescence. Gu et al. [32] derived CDs from wolfberries that reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+. Simultaneously, the carbon dots form surface defects, resulting in failed electron transfer, causing fluorescence quenching upon the addition of Fe3+. However, the addition of ascorbic acid to this system restores the surface defects of carbon dots caused by Fe3+, exhibiting fluorescence recovery. Gu et al. synthesized CDs from Lycium barbarum. After adding Fe3+ into the CDs solution, Fe3+ was reduced to Fe2+ by CDs, and at the same time, CDs formed surface defects, which led to electron transfer failure and led to fluorescence quenching. After adding ascorbic acid to the system, the surface defects of CDs were restored, so that the fluorescence was recovered. Ma et al. [33] utilized activated carbon as the carbon source to synthesize yellow fluorescent CDs in a strongly acidic medium. The resulting fluorescence was quenched by Cu2+ and then restored by adding ascorbic acid to the quenched system. This restoration of fluorescence by the ascorbic acid happened because it reduced Cu2+ to Cu+, leading to possible degradation of the CD-Cu complex. Currently, there are also several methods related to MnO4 - detection, mainly utilizing the inner filter effect. [34–36].

In the present study, firstly, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield were synthesized. Because there are many negative groups on their surfaces, they can coordinate with Cu2+, and nitrogen-doped carbon dots-copper complexes(NCQDs@Cu) with low fluorescence quantum yield were obtained. Subsequently MnO4 - oxidized the groups with coordination ability in the NCQDs, which improved the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs@Cu system, and there was a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity increment and MnO4 - content. This new type of CDs-Ion realizes the quantitative detection of MnO4 - in explosives.

2,3-diaminopyridine, anhydrous ethanol, NaF, NaClO4, NaClO3, KNO3, NaBrO3, KBr, Na2SO3, CuSO4, NaNO2, Na2SO4, NaCl, K2Cr2O7, BaCl2, KCl, FeCl3, Pb(NO3)2, ZnCl2, MnCl2, Ca(NO3)2, NH4Cl, NiCl3, and MgCl2 were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Trinitrotoluene (TNT), Picric acid (PA), Hexogen (RDX), pentaerythritol tetranitrate (PETN), and tetryl were obtained from the sample repository of the Criminal Investigation Police University of China. Unless otherwise specified, all reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the NCQDs and NCQDs@Cu
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare NCQDs, 0.5 g of 2,3-diaminopyridine was mixed with 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol and sonicated for 20 min after stirring with a glass rod. The solution was then transferred to a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 10 h in an oven. After cooling, the product mixture was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was filtered through a nylon membrane (0.44 μm). The filtrate was contained in a 3500 Da dialysis bag and immersed in ultrapure water for 12 h. Subsequently, the reddish-brown solution inside the bag was freeze-dried, yielding 225 mg of dark red carbon dot powder (NCQDs).

For the preparation of the NCQDs-Cu complex, 100 mg of the as-prepared NCQDs was dissolved in 10 mL of water and dropwise added with 3 mL of 0.5 mol/L CuSO4 under constant magnetic stirring. After stirring for 1 h, the mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, resulting in a black precipitate. The precipitate was washed three times with water and dried at 100 °C in a drying oven for 3 h, yielding 112 mg of black solid powder (NCQDs@Cu).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the NCQDs and NCQDs@Cu
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence measurements were conducted using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (G9800A, Agilent Technologies, USA) with a 10 mm quartz cuvette. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU Corporation, Japan) with a 10 mm quartz cell. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed using a Nicolet iS50 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) in transmission mode with KBr pellets. The morphology and size of the prepared carbon quantum dots were determined using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Hitachi-600, Hitachi, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using a PHI 5702 spectrometer (Thermo scientific K-Alpha, America).

A stock solution of NCQDs@Cu (0.5 mg/mL) was prepared by dispersing 50 mg of the powder in 50 mL of water through sonication and diluting it to 100 mL. This solution was stored at 5 °C in a refrigerator for further use.

For selectivity evaluation, a NCQDs@Cu solution (20 mg·L-1), MnO4 - solution (5 μmol/L) and an interference (50 μmol/L) [NaF, NaClO4, NaClO3, KNO3, NaBrO3, KBr, Na2SO3, NaNO2, Na2SO4, NaCl, K2Cr2O7, BaCl2, KCl, FeCl3, Pb(NO3)2, ZnCl2, MnCl2, Ca(NO3)2, NH4Cl, NiCl3, Na2S, or H2O2 in water, or TNT, PA, RDX, PETN, or Tetryl in DMSO] were mixed with phosphate buffered solution (PBS, 10 mmol/L) at pH 7.0. The solution was allowed to stand for 5 min before fluorescence measurements.

For fluorescence response, a NCQDs@Cu solution (20 mg·L-1 and various MnO4 - concentrations (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5, 4.0, 4.5, 5.0, 7.0, 10.0 μmol·L-1) were mixed with PBS buffer solution at pH 7.0. The solutions were allowed to stand for 5 min before fluorescence measurements.
All the fluorescence measurements were conducted at an excitation wavelength of 430 nm.

Samples of simulated potassium permanganate-sucrose explosives were prepared by homogeneously mixing potassium permanganate and sucrose. A 5 g sample of simulated potassium permanganate-sucrose explosive containing 50.0 %, 33.3 % and 10.0 % potassium permanganate was obtained by homogeneously mixing potassium permanganate with sucrose.
A real sample from an explosion in 2019 was tested in the study. This explosive dust (100.0 g) was extracted and stored in the sample bank of the Criminal Investigation Police University of China.
An extensive investigation was carried out on the preparation of the NCQDs and NCQDs@Cu to optimize the performance. The preparation processeis depicted in Fig. 1 .

The precursor amount profoundly affected the fluorescence of the prepared NCQDs. Fluorescence intensity increased with increasing amount of precursor (0.2, 0.5, 1.0 g). Although using 0.2 g of substrate led to higher fluorescence intensity in NCQDs, it posed difficulties in forming NCQDs@Cu precipitates in subsequent reactions. Using 1 g of substrate resulted in an excess of 2,3-diaminopyridine, complicating further processing. The effects of solvent (water, ethanol, and DMF) on the flourenscnece intensity were also accessed (Fig. S1). It was found that NCQDs synthesized in water and DMF exhibited relatively weaker fluorescence compared to ethanol, which was selected for the subsequent study. The reaction temperature affected the fluorescence intensity as well. Among the various temperatures (180, 200, 220, and 240 ℃), the NCQDs synthesized at 220 ℃ exhibited the optimal fluorescence intensity, as shown in Fig. S2. For the reaction time (4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 h), the fluorescence intensity and subsequent NCQDs@Cu yields showed marginal differences beyond 12 h, as shown in Fig. S3. Therefore, 10 h was selected as the optimal synthesis time for NCQDs in the subsequent study.

In the subsequent preparation of NCQDs@Cu, we examined the effects of the concentration and volume of copper sulfate solution on the fluorescence characteristics of the material. Considering convenience during preparation, we examined relatively higher concentrations of copper sulfate solution, i.e., 0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 mol·L-1. The macroscopic appearance of NCQDs@Cu formed with 0.1 and 0.5 mol·L-1 copper sulfate solutions was similar, whereas the NCQDs@Cu precipitated faster and tended to aggregate when 1.0 mol/L copper sulfate solution was used. Therefore, we selected 0.5 mol·L-1 as the optimum copper sulfate solution. For the reaction temperature, we found no significant difference between the reaction conducted under heating and at room temperature. Moreover, precipitation of NCQDs@Cu ceased after 1 h under both conditions, establishing a 1-hour stirring period at room temperature as the optimum condition (Fig. S4). It was also worth noting that uniform spreading of the NCQDs@Cu precipitate during drying was crucial for effective dissolution in the buffer solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical characterization of the NCQDs and NCQDs@Cu
ce:section.ce:para: The NCQDs and NCQDs@Cu are dispersed uniformly in both water. Fig. 2 a shows the NCQDs under TEM. These presented a spherical morphology, with particle sizes ranging from 3.5 to 4.5 nm, averaging at ∼ 4.2 nm (Fig. 2a inset). As such, the particles appeared highly uniform, which could lead to excitation-independent fluorescence[37]. High-resolution TEM images (Fig. 2a inset) revealed lattice fringes of graphitic carbon (sp2) with a spacing of ∼ 0.23 nm, indicating the crystalline nature of the prepared NCQDs [38].

FTIR spectra of the NCQDs exhibited peaks at 3360 cm−1 and 3180 cm−1, attributed to –NH bending vibrations, while the peak at 1630 cm−1 corresponds to C=O stretching vibrations[32]. The C=C stretching vibration was also evident at 1250 cm−1, with the additional peaks at 1467 cm−1 attributed to C-N stretching vibrations (Fig. 2b), respectively[39]. XPS revealed the elemental composition of the NCQDs, with peaks at 284, 399, and 530 eV representing C1s, N1s, and O1s, as shown in Fig. 2c. The relative intensities indicated that the NCQDs contained carbon (65.78 %), oxygen (9.4 %), and nitrogen (24.83 %) according to the number of atoms. Furthermore, the resolved spectra (Fig. 2d ∼ f) of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s showed peaks at 284.7, 285.4, and 286.5 eV, attributed to C=C, C–C, and C-N bonds, respectively[40]. On the other hand, the N 1 s spectrum comprised of four sub-peaks representing pyridinic N (398.6 eV), amino N (399.1 eV), pyrrolic N (399.9 eV), and graphitic N (400.8 eV)[41]. Moreover, oxygen existed in C=O and C–O forms, as evidenced by the peaks at 531.6 eV and 532.3 eV, respectively [40].

The results indicated that under hydrothermal conditions, 2,3-diaminopyridine followed complex polymerization, intermolecular cyclization, and condensation reactions [42]. These also revealed the presence of amino and hydroxyl functional groups on the NCQDs, which can participate in coordination reactions [43].

Introducing Cu into the NCQDs resulted in significant changes in the FTIR, EDS and XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. S5, the FTIR of NCQDs and NCQDs@Cu were compared. After reaction with Cu2+, the C=O and C-N bending modes were weakened at 1630 and 1467 cm−1, respectively. The distinct peaks at 1147, 1086 cm−1 and 630 cm−1 were attributed to the stretch vibration bands of SO4 2-, S=O and S-O, respectively[44]. These changes indicated the formation of coordination between the NCQDs and copper [45]. In addition, Fig. S6 presents the Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis for NCQDs@Cu. The spectrum clearly identifies the presence of copper (Cu) and sulfur (S) elements, which corroborates the effective doping of copper within the NCQDs@Cu composite. XPS showed that, compared to NCQDs, NCQDs@Cu had distinct Cu-related peaks (932.5 eV and 953.2 eV representing Cu 2p1/2 and 2p2/3, respectively)[46] and an elemental composition of 73.04 %, 11.46 %, 11.88 %, 2.72 %, and 0.02 % for C, N, O, Cu and S according to the number of atoms, respectively (Fig. 3 a). At high resolution (Fig. 3b ∼ f), the Cu 2p peak revealed the presence of Cu-N (397.9 eV), Cu-O (530.3 eV) [47] and Cu+ (932.5 eV and 953.2 eV) [48,49]species on the surface of the NCQDs@Cu. In the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 3f), two sulfur species at 168.3 and 169.8 eV were assigned to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 locations of the −SOx. These findings suggest a chemical reaction between the NCQDs and Cu2+, resulting in the formation of NCQDs@Cu complex. Therefore, it can be proposed that Cu2+ was reduced by the NCQDs and coordinated with the nitrogen and oxygen functional groups present on the NCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characteristics of the NCQDs and NCQDs@Cu
ce:section.ce:para: The 3D-fluorescence, UV–Vis absorption, Excitation, and Emission spectra of NCQDs, NCQDs@Cu, and NCQDs@Cu/MnO4 - were recorded and shown in Figs. S7, S8 and S9. With pure water as the solvent and the excitation wavelength ranging from 320 to 500 nm, the NCQDs displayed a predominant emission peak around 520 nm independent of the excitation wavelength (Fig. S7a). When excited at 430 nm, NCQDs emitted light centered around 520 nm and exhibited bright yellow-green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. S7b inset right). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the NCQDs (Fig. S7b) displayed three characteristic absorption peaks at 243, 315, and 430 nm. These peaks at 243 nm and 315 nm correspond to π–π* transitions in the aromatic network and n–π* transitions of the C=O/C=N bonds, respectively [50]. On the other hand, the absorption band around 430 nm showed a clear overlap with the excitation wavelength of the NCQDs, it is regarded as a broad absorption on the surface of NCQDs[51]. Furthermore, the Quantum yield (QY) of the NCQDs was calculated to be 27.7 % (Table S1). On the contrary, the NCQDs@Cu exhibited very weak optical properties due to coordination, as indicated by the small QY value of about 2.2 % (Table S1). However, interestingly, the QY of the NCQDs@Cu was significantly enhanced to 10.2 % after the addition of MnO4 - (Fig. S7c and d, and Table S1). This system exhibited an emission wavelength of 520 nm (excitation wavelength of 430 nm) and appeared bright green under 365 nm UV irradiation (Fig. S7d inset right). Moreover, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the system (NCQDs@Cu/MnO4 -) mainly featured an absorption peak at 280 nm and a widely absorption band in the range of 390–500 nm (Fig. S7b). This comprehensive analysis of the 3D-fluorescence, UV–Vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra determined the excitation wavelength of 430 nm for the NCQDs@Cu/MnO4 - system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the optical characteristics of NCQDs, NCQDs@Cu and NCQDs@Cu/MnO4
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of NCQDs, NCQDs@Cu, and NCQDs@Cu/MnO4 - measured at different pH values ranging from 2 to 12 are shown in Fig. S10. It indicated that the fluorescence intensity of the NCQDs at 520 nm (Fig. S10 point) varied with the pH and showed the highest intensity at a pH of 6. This observation suggests that the variation in pH possibly influenced the functional groups on the surface of the NCQDs, altering the electron energies[52]. NCQDs@Cu exhibited higher fluorescence intensity at stronger acidic pH, suggesting protonation of the amine groups on the surface of NCQDs, which reduces their coordination ability with Cu+. Upon adding MnO4 -, the fluorescence intensity decreased due to the strong oxidative property of MnO4 - destroyed the conjugated structure on the NCQDs in highly acidic conditions. It is speculated that in alkaline environments, hydrolysis of Cu+ in NCQDs@Cu leads to separation from NCQDs, thereby resulting in increased initial fluorescence intensity. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs@Cu remained stable at pH 6 ∼ 11 (Fig. S10 squareness). For the NCQDs@Cu/MnO4 - system, the fluorescence recovery (Fig. S10 triangle) was the most significant at pH∼7.0. Consequently, the PBS buffer solution of pH of 7.0 (10 mmol·L-1) was selected for the test experiments of the samples (simulated and real explosives).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response Time, Selectivity, and anti-interference of NCQDs@Cu for MnO4
ce:section.ce:para: The response time, selectivity, and anti-interference capabilities yielded satisfactory results, as shown in Figs. S11 and S12. For instance, 20 mg·L-1 NCQDs@Cu showed response times of ∼ 2.5, ∼ 2.0, and ∼ 0.5 min for 1.0, 3.0, and 5.0 μmol·L-1 MnO4 -, respectively. Thus, 5 min was allowed for testing the simulated and real explosives in the following sections. The selectivity and anti-interference capability of the NCQDs@Cu (20 mg·L-1) for MnO4 - (5 μmol·L-1) were evaluated in the presence of 50 μmol·L-1 of various possible interferences [including common ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, F-, Br-, Cl-, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, NO2 –), non-standard explosive residual ions (S2-, ClO4 -, NO3 –, ClO3 -, Ba2+, Pb2+, NH4 +), oxidants (BrO3 -, Cr2O7 -, H2O2), and organic explosives (TNT, PA, RDX, PENT, Tetryl)], as shown in Fig. S10, only addition of the MnO4 - induced significant spectral changes of the NCQDs@Cu solution, indicating that NCQDs@Cu was highly selective for MnO4 -. While the non-standard explosive residual ions (ClO4 -, NO3 –, ClO3 -) and oxidants (BrO3 -, Cr2O7 -, H2O2) also have the oxidizing ability, these species only induced slight changes to the spectrum of NCQDs@Cu solution under 10-fold concentration conditions of MnO4 -. Moreover, in the presence of the interferences, the MnO4 - resulted in a similar response exhibiting almost no interference in the presence of other competing analytes (Fig. S10). The results suggested that NCQDs@Cu had high selectivity and anti-interference ability with a great promise for detecting MnO4 - under complex conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence response of NCQDs@Cu for MnO4
ce:section.ce:para: The quantitative fluorescence response of NCQDs@Cu (20 mg·L-1) for different concentrations of MnO4 - (0–5 μmol·L-1) is shown in Fig. 4 a. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity gradually increases with the addition of MnO4 -, exhibiting a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs@Cu solution and MnO4 - concentration (0–5 μmol·L-1). This relationship was evaluated as PL=112.6[C] + 112.6, where PL represents the relative fluorescence intensity, and [C] denotes the concentration of MnO4 - in the system. Employing the 3σ rule[53], the detection limit of NCQDs@Cu was calculated to be ∼ 32.4 nmol·L-1 MnO4 -, indicating a high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of the NCQDs@Cu for MnO4
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical characterization (in section 3.2) confirmed the presence of a significant amount of Cu+-N/Cu+-O in the NCQDs@Cu complex. These coordinated active sites may undergo reactions under strong oxidative conditions, breaking the coordination and restoring the characteristic fluorescence of the NCQDs.[54] To confirm this, we investigated the NCQDs@Cu after reacting with MnO4 - through XPS, FTIR, and fluorescence spectroscopy.

XPS shows that compared with NCQDs@Cu, (Fig. 5 ), the XPS of copper has changed significantly after adding MnO4 -, the Cu 2p spectrum comprised of four sub-peaks (964.6, 957.3, 944.6,936.4 eV). Which means that the valence state of copper changes from Cu+ to Cu2+ due to the oxidation of MnO4 -. On the other hand, the half-peak width of nitrogen is obviously widened(Fig. 5f), which indicates that nitro groups are produced (the peak of Oxidizes-N for 403.6 eV, in the Fig. 5c, the peak of N-O for 533.9 eV, in the Fig. 5d).

FTIR spectra of the NCQDs@Cu for MnO4 - exhibited strong peaks at 1597 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1, attributed to carbonyl and nitro group bending vibrations, suggesting that part of the hydroxyl groups and amino groups on the surface of NCQDs were oxidized into carbonyl and nitro groups (Fig. S13). Furthermore, the fluorescence spectrum of the post-reaction solution revealed a 10 nm blue shift in the emission wavelength compared to the pristine NCQDs, associated with a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S14). It suggests that some structures of the NCQDs were also oxidized during the process, preventing the complete recovery of fluorescence intensity.[55] At the same time, we added copper ions into the system of adding MnO4 - to NCQDs@Cu, and found that it did not undergo fluorescence quenching (Fig. S14). It is proved that the oxidized NCQDs@Cu can no longer coordinate with Cu2+ to induce quenching, which also shows that the structure of NCQDs has changed.

According to the results, it can be considered that the introduction of MnO4 - oxidizes Cu+ in the NCQDs@Cu complex and nucleophilic amino/hydroxyl groups on NCQDs, resulting in a significant enhancement of the fluorescence of NCQDs@Cu (Fig. S15).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The simulated and real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The simulated and actual explosive samples were first standardized for the KMnO4 concentration using the national standard method (GB/T 1608–2017). These samples were then tested using the prepared NCQDs@Cu fluorescence sensor. A comparison of the results in Table 1 indicates that the two quantitative methods were consistent. However, when the concentration of KMnO4 is low, the national standard method adopts a complicated back-titration method. In comparison, this new method based on NCQDs@Cu fluorescence is more convenient and has a strong detection ability for real explosive samples.

Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs), using 2,3-diaminopyridine as a carbon and nitrogen source, were successfully synthesized via a simple solvothermal method. The resulting spherical NCQDs with 4.2 nm size and graphene-like structure exhibited a significantly high fluorescence quantum yield (27.3 %), sensitive to pH changes. Leveraging the abundant amino groups on the surface of NCQDs, Cu2+ was successfully complexed with it to form NCQDs@Cu for the detection of MnO4. In the presence of MnO4 -, the NCQDs@Cu exhibited certain redox reactions, disrupting the coordination state of NCQDs@Cu and recovering the fluorescence in about 5 min. The fluorescence recovery/enhancement showed a linear relationship with the MnO4 - concentration, making it suitable for quantitative analysis. The study, thus, introduced a novel method for quantifying MnO4 - in the range of 0––5 μmol·L-1 and a detection limit of 32.4 nmol·L-1. Consequently, the technique was successfully applied to inspect KMnO4 content in non-standard explosives, demonstrating its reliability compared to the conventional titration methods.

Furthermore, the underlying principle of this method presents intriguing new prospects for developing carbon dot-metal complex-based chemosensors, inspiring future research. In the future research, we look forward to further optimizing the NCQDs@Cu, making it into test paper or optical sensor array, and applying it in the field of explosive detection.

Huichuan Yu: Writing – original draft. Hongda Li: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yue Zhang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804140), and the Special Project for Strengthening the Basic Work of Police by Science and Technology of the Ministry of Public Security (No. 2022JC02), and Shenyang Young and Middle-aged Science and Technology Innovation Talents Support Program (No. RC220480).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111422.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
